I have Js9c on 2d8s6h. I have an open-ended straight draw (5,6,7,8,9,T,J - wait: 7 or T makes my straight). Actually with J9 on 268: I need a T for J-T-9-8... no. 6-7-8-9-T would need a 7 and T... J9 on 2-8-6: I need T for T-J... no straight draw directly. Wait: 9-8 are connected. With J-9 and board 2-8-6: 7-8-9 needs a 7, or 9-T-J needs a T. 

So I'm looking at gutshots here - a 7 completes 6-7-8-9, and a T completes 9-T-J, though the T would give me an open-ended if it hits. I've got some overcards and backdoor possibilities, but mostly I'm working with these two-card outs. Since villain folds to cbets 58% of the time and I'm the preflop aggressor, this is a solid spot to bet despite my draw being somewhat limited.

Villain's likely holding at least a pair given the action. I'm looking at 8 outs for a straight with the sevens and tens, but pot odds require about 29% equity to call and I'm only getting roughly 17% with those outs. This is a fold.

content.thinking:
Opponent is loose-passive (VPIP 59%, PFR 16%). They raised here preflop - that's within their 16% range so it's not necessarily strong.

I have 8s7s - a good suited connector. Villain has only 164 chips total, raised to 14. Their stack is 164, pot is 16, they've put in 14 already so they have 150 behind.

With 8s7s, I can call. The pot odds: need to call 12 to win pot of 28 (including my call) = 12/28 = 43% equity needed. 

My suited connector has solid equity against their loose-passive range, and with villain down to 150 chips after their raise, I'm considering whether to 3-bet as a semi-bluff. They might fold postflop despite their loose tendencies, but their preflop raise suggests they could have something decent. If I shove all-in, I'm committing their remaining stack.

Actually, calling makes more sense here—I can take the flop and leverage their 57% fold rate to c-bets, which gives me a profitable spot with 8s7s.

content.text:
Key observations:
- Villain is loose-passive: VPIP 59%, PFR 16% — this preflop raise is in range but not super strong
- Villain folds to flop c-bets 57% (8/14) — very exploitable post-flop
- Villain folds to river bets 100% (4/4) — extreme river passivity
- Short stack: only 164 chips, so 150 behind after their raise

With **8s7s** (strong suited connector), I have great playability. Calling 12 into a 28-chip pot is solid: I can c-bet villain off many hands postflop given their high fold-to-cbet rate, and I have good implied odds + equity with two suited connected cards.

content.thinking:
Villain raised to 100, I need to call 80 more. Pot is 148, villain has 64 behind.

I have OESD (6 or T gives straight). If I call 80, pot becomes 228, villain has 64 behind. 

Pot odds: 80 to win 228 = need ~35% equity. With OESD I have 8 outs = ~32% on two streets, but if I call here villain is basically all-in almost (64 behind). 

Actually if I raise all-in, villain has to call 64 more into a huge pot. Let me think about shoving - min raise is 180, max 608. 

If I shove, villain needs to call 64 more into a pot around 300+, which gives them great odds so they'll likely call. My OESD is roughly 32% equity against their likely top pair+ range. Running the math on calling: I pay 80 now, face a 64 river bet, and my expected value works out to about -30 chips.

Wait, let me recalculate the pot odds more carefully. I'm calling 80 into a pot of 228, so I need 35% equity to break even. But with just one card to come, my OESD is only 17% to hit. That's well short of the 35% I need. Even accounting for the 64 chips I can win from villain if I hit on the turn, the math still comes out to roughly -30. Folding looks like the right play here.

Though I should consider that villain might be bluffing or semi-bluffing given the short stack. But given the action—raising preflop and then raising the c-bet on an ace-high board—villain almost certainly has an ace or a strong hand. I'm folding.

content.text:
Villain check-raised my c-bet hard on an A-high board. With only one card to come, my OESD gives ~17% equity (8 outs). Pot odds require ~35% — even with villain's 64 behind as implied odds, the math is losing. Villain's line (PFR preflop + raise on A-high flop) screams an ace. Fold is correct.
